'Particularly' is an adverbial modifier (intensifier/specificity marker) that expresses gradation, scope narrowing, or emphasis — functions that require modal or degree operators outside the prime lexicon. The four senses from Wiktionary (emphasis, magnitude, specificity, detail-focus) all require meta-linguistic or propositional operators ('very', 'only', 'in particular about X') that collapse into role-fillers or modifiers within existing predicates rather than standing as independent concepts. It is a grammatical functor, not a concrete thing, percept, action, or state expressible via the InstanceSchema/WhenTrue/RelationalSchema frames with the available primes and clausal operators.

'terribly' is an adverb that modifies predicates or other adverbs to express degree (very/extremely) or manner (in a way that causes fear). Its core function is to intensify or qualify other concepts rather than to name a thing, kind, place, body part, or percept. While the intensifier 'very' appears in the allowed lexicon as a modifier within SPs, 'terribly' as a free adverb operating over arbitrary predicates cannot be grounded in a molecule (which requires a concrete anchor) and its meaning as a degree modifier is not directly expressible as an explication because it presupposes a host predicate. It belongs to the grammar/modifier tier, not the semantic-primitive tier.

'el' is a homonym with multiple senses: a language code (ISO 639-1 symbol), the name of a letter in multiple scripts (Latin, Cyrillic), a numeral (11 in duodecimal), an elevated railway (Chicago slang), and an article in other languages. None of these has a single 'dominant everyday sense' in English; they are technical labels, proper names, script/numeral conventions, or language-specific grammatical words. The most frequent colloquial sense in English-speaking contexts is the Chicago elevated railway, but that is a proper noun referring to a specific system, not a concept with stable universal grounding. The letter-name senses are metalinguistic conventions (names of symbols), not grounded in perception or action. This concept requires disambiguation by register/context and belongs to a symbolic or technical stratum rather than the everyday semantic primitives available in this lexicon.

'practically' is an adverb denoting manner, modality, or degree of truth across a clause (in practice; almost entirely; feasibly). Its dominant everyday senses — 'in effect though not officially' and 'almost completely' — are meta-logical operators over propositions or quantified statements, not grounded in the prime lexicon's concrete percepts, actions, or states. A proper formalization would require operators for 'APPROXIMATELY', 'DE-FACTO', or 'MODAL-QUALIFICATION' that are not available in kot-ast/1. The concept belongs to the logical/pragmatic layer, not the semantic-primitive tier.

Bible (the religious text) is a culturally-bound artefact whose identity conditions depend on historical, textual, and theological criteria that lie outside the prime lexicon. While one could ground 'a book' or 'words', the concept of 'Bible' as a sacred, authoritative text of a specific religious tradition requires reference to Christianity, Judaism, or Islam—cultural-historical kinds that are not primitives in the kernel. The figurative sense ('a comprehensive manual') is derivative and semantically parasitic on the sacred sense. Neither is expressible using only {SOMETHING~THING, WORDS, PEOPLE, kind, part} and the allowed primes without smuggling in proper nouns or religion-specific predicates.

Religion is a complex social-cultural system involving beliefs about supernatural/transcendental realities, organized practices, ethics, worldviews, and institutions. While its components (belief, ritual, community, sacred places) are individually graspable in primes, the concept as a unified phenomenon—the systematic integration of metaphysical worldview with social organization, moral codes, and commemorative practice—requires reference to abstract institutional and ideological structures ('supernatural reality', 'spiritual element', 'sacred') that exceed the capacity of the prime lexicon. The concept is not anchored to a single concrete thing, kind, body part, percept, action, artefact, place, substance, or time. Its core sense is inherently a theoretical abstraction over social systems, not a molecule-grounded percept or a simple relation expressible in primes.

'Jewish' is a proper ethnoreligious category anchored in historical, cultural, and religious identity that involves complex social conventions, legal membership criteria (halakha, conversion protocols), and collective self-identification spanning millennia. Its core meaning cannot be grounded in the prime lexicon (which admits only concrete percepts, bodily actions, simple relations, and mental states) or expressed as a molecule over the available catalog. The concept requires reference to specific historical communities, religious texts, conversion law, and cultural practice — all outside the scope of the Kernel's semantic primitives. It belongs to the social-history and religion sectors, not this tier.

'Frankly' is an adverbial discourse marker expressing the speaker's epistemic stance or manner of assertion ('in a candid way', 'to tell the truth', 'plainly evident'). It modifies clauses or entire utterances rather than picking out a concrete thing, kind, body part, percept, action, artefact, place, substance, or time. While it expresses a mental/communicative attitude, it is not a pure mental state or speech act amenable to explication in the prime lexicon—it is fundamentally a pragmatic operator on discourse. The catalog lacks the necessary frame and predicates to capture its function as a listener-directed truthfulness signal or manner adverbial over arbitrary propositional content.

'Fifth' is fundamentally a grammar word — it expresses a position in an ordered sequence by reference to the number five. Its identity depends on counting and ordinality, which are not grounded in the prime lexicon (no FIVE prime, no COUNT or ORDER predicate). The everyday senses (ordinal adjective, positional noun, fractional quantity) all pivot on the abstract concept of numerical sequence. While 'one-fifth' as a fractional proportion could in principle be expressed via division, 'fifth' as the ordinal form is a pure counting word, not a thing, action, percept, or state expressible without explicit number semantics. This belongs to the mathematical/grammatical tier, not the semantic-primitive tier.

'everyday' is fundamentally a modifier or comparative property (ordinary, commonplace, recurring) that depends on contrast with non-ordinary or special occasions. While 'ordinary' or 'common' might be expressible as frequency or typicality predicates, 'everyday' as used in English requires either a relational frame (X is everyday *relative to* special occasions) or a modal/comparative operator (X happens in the normal course) that are not available in the prime lexicon. The concept is primarily adjectival and collocational (everyday life, everyday object) rather than a grounded thing, kind, or event. It cannot be anchored to a single concrete molecule, and its explication would require comparative or normative primitives outside the allowed schema.

vampire is a mythological/fictional creature whose identity conditions (undeath, supernatural feeding on vital essence, immunity to normal death) cannot be grounded in the prime lexicon or catalog. The everyday sense conflates a folkloric supernatural being with metaphorical usage (energy drain) and a rare medical condition. None of these senses are anchored in perceivable actions, bodily states, or concrete artifacts expressible in primes; the concept belongs to the fantasy/folklore domain, not the everyday physical/social tier this lexicon covers.

'Classic' is a graded evaluative predicate (excellence, timelessness, exemplariness, cultural standing) whose truth conditions depend on culturally variable standards of taste, historical judgment, and aesthetic canon. The concept requires either modal operators (something 'should be' or 'will be' remembered) or comparative/superlative framing ('better than most', 'will last longer') that exceed the prime lexicon. While kernel concepts like {urn:kernel-v0:useful|useful} and {urn:kernel-v0:helpful|helpful} exist for basic instrumental value, 'classic' involves diachronic cultural evaluation—does this work outlast fashion? does this exemplify its kind for future people?—which cannot be grounded in immediate perceptual or bodily primitives or expressed in a closed explication without importing undefined meta-predicates. The molecule route fails (no stable percept or artefact identity conditions). The explication route requires quantifiers over time, taste-communities, and canonical status that the prime system does not provide.

'Forth' is a directional adverb expressing motion or extension away from a reference point (forward in time/place/degree, or out into view). Its core meaning is relational and comparative—it denotes direction relative to an implicit observer or starting state. While 'forward', 'out', and 'away' are spatial/temporal relations, 'forth' itself is a closed-class grammar word (preposition/adverb) whose sense is exhausted by its role in marking trajectory. It cannot be grounded in the molecule lexicon (which requires concrete things, kinds, body parts, percepts, actions, artefacts, places, substances, or kinship), nor can it be fully expressed in the prime vocabulary without reducing to metalinguistic description of its own relational role. The concept belongs to the closed-class grammatical tier, not to the semantic-primitive tier.

'beth' is the name of a letter — a conventional sign in a writing system. Its identity is determined by its position in an alphabetic sequence and its graphic/phonetic role in languages like Hebrew, Aramaic, Phoenician, and Syriac. This is a proper name for a cultural artefact (a grapheme) whose essence depends on linguistic-historical convention, not on perceivable physical properties or actions expressible in the prime lexicon. The everyday sense is 'the letter called beth in these languages', but that sense is fundamentally a label, not a grounded concept.

'Electric' is a technical-physics adjective whose core sense depends on the concept of electric charge and electromagnetic phenomena. These are not expressible in the prime lexicon or as grounding notes over the available catalog. The everyday uses (e.g. 'an electric car' = 'a car powered by electricity') are derivative on the physics sense and would require either a molecule for 'electricity' (itself physics-grounded, depth too deep) or direct reference to electromagnetic phenomena that fall outside the semantic primitives. The emotional sense ('electrifying') is metaphorical. This belongs to the physics/technology sector, not this tier.

The dominant everyday sense of 'behalf' is a relational-grammatical functor ('on behalf of X', 'in behalf of Y') that expresses agency or representation — someone acts, speaks, or decides in the stead or interest of another. This is fundamentally a speech-act and social-role relation that cannot be grounded as a concrete thing, kind of thing, body part, percept, bodily action, artefact, place, substance, or time. It also cannot be fully expressed using the bare primes (THINK, SAY, DO, WANT, etc.) without circularity: to explicate 'on behalf of' requires either a metalinguistic account of what 'behalf' itself means, or recourse to agency and intentionality structures that exceed the closed lexicon and kernel predicates. The Wiktionary sense 'interest, benefit, or wellbeing' is secondary and metaphorical; the Wikipedia sense is a proper name (Behalf Inc., a fintech company) and not a concept. This belongs to the pragmatics/semantics of social relations, not the tier of semantic primitives.

berlin (the carriage) requires expression of wheeled vehicles, structural parts (wheel, axle, body, hood), and the notion of covering/enclosure. None of these have semantic primitives in the ALLOWED vocabulary or molecules in the catalog. The word 'wheel' is a technical artifact term, not a semantic primitive; 'covered' and 'separate' are absent from ALLOWED. The carriage type cannot be grounded in the current kernel vocabulary. The other sense (Berlin the city) is a proper name and thus out of scope.

'incredibly' is an adverbial intensifier and discourse particle expressing degree, surprise, or epistemic stance ('to a great extent', 'in a manner hard to believe', 'used to note surprising truth'). These functions are not expressible in the prime lexicon or kot-ast/1 grammar: the core meaning is a meta-comment on the believability or intensity of a proposition, not a grounded entity, action, property, or event that can be anchored to the ALLOWED vocabulary or catalog concepts. Intensifiers and evidentiality markers belong to the grammar/pragmatics tier, not the semantic-primitive tier.

'Downtown' is a culture-bound, geographically situated term denoting a human-defined urban zone (the central business district of a North American city). Its identity conditions rest on social/economic/historical facts about cities, commerce, and civic organization — none of which map to the prime lexicon (PEOPLE, PLACE, THING, etc.) in a stable way. The concept requires grounding in 'city', 'business', 'center', and 'commerce', none of which are available as molecules or kernel concepts, and all of which are themselves composite social artifacts. A grounding note would need to say 'a place where people do business in a city center', but 'city' and 'business' are not primitive percepts or natural kinds; they are institutional constructs. This belongs to the urban-geography and economics sectors, not the tier reachable by the Kernel lexicon.

The dominant everyday sense of 'thus' is an adverbial connective or discourse operator meaning 'in this way' or 'as a result / therefore'. This is a grammatical / logical connective word, not a content concept (thing, kind, action, or property). It belongs to the closed class of logical operators and sequencing particles, not to the open vocabulary of the Kernel of Truth catalogue. The noun sense (thuris, a rune name) is a proper noun / technical term outside everyday semantic grounding.

The dominant everyday sense of 'punk' in contemporary English is a cultural-identity and aesthetic concept: a young person (or their style, music, attitude) associated with anti-establishment rebellion, DIY ethos, specific fashion (spiked hair, leather, safety pins), and punk rock music. This identity is grounded in mid-20th-century social history, subcultural affiliation, and stylistic convention—none of which can be expressed in the prime lexicon or anchored to the available catalog (which contains only concrete things, basic actions, emotions, and kinship). The Wiktionary senses listed are archaic or derogatory slang with narrow referents; they do not capture the modern dominant sense. Punk-as-cultural-identity requires reference to specific historical moment, music genre, fashion codes, and social attitude—all outside the scope of this tier.

'Greek' in the dominant everyday sense refers to the language or people of Greece, which is a proper-noun category (ethnonym + language name). The Wiktionary senses provided—gibberish, a sexual act, placeholder text, template-filling, logo obscuring—are all specialized jargon (typography, graphic design, web development) or slang derived from the proper noun. None are stable everyday concepts expressible in primes. The language itself (as a code system for communication) would require technical grounding beyond the prime lexicon. Ethnonyms and language names belong to the proper-noun tier, not the semantic-primitive tier.

'Pope' is a proper institutional role within a specific historical and religious tradition (Roman Catholicism), defined by ecclesiastical authority structures, theological claims (apostolic succession, papal infallibility), and political sovereignty over Vatican City State. Its identity conditions rest on cultural, legal, and doctrinal facts that cannot be grounded in the prime lexicon (PEOPLE, SOMEONE, THINK, KNOW, DO, etc.) or the available catalog concepts. The everyday sense conflates a religious office, a claim to infallibility, a territorial sovereign role, and metaphorical uses ('absolute authority'). None of these decompose into the semantic primes without smuggling in the very institutional knowledge the system is designed to avoid. This belongs to the religion/history/governance sector, not the tier-1 grounding vocabulary.

'Organization' in its dominant everyday sense denotes a social institution with internal structure, distributed agency, formal purposes, and written governance — concepts that require stable identity over time, intentional coordination across multiple agents with different roles, and abstract social commitment (rules, membership, hierarchy). These are not expressible in the prime lexicon: there is no prime for 'role', 'rule', 'member', 'purpose' as distinct from simple WANT, no prime for formal authority or legitimacy, and no way to capture the self-sustaining institutional identity that persists independent of any single person's presence. A minimal explication would require at least five new semantic molecules (e.g., 'rule', 'member', 'role', 'authority', 'purpose') before organization itself could be grounded. This belongs to the social-structure sector, not the kernel tier.

'knight' denotes a social-institutional role defined by medieval feudal relations, formal rank conferral, and historical military practices. Its identity conditions depend on: (1) membership in a specific socio-legal system (feudalism, monarchy, state honours); (2) a formal grant or ceremony from an authority; (3) cultural/historical context that anchors meaning to specific geopolitical structures. None of these can be expressed in the prime lexicon (which covers only actions, perceptions, simple relations, kinship, concrete objects, and basic properties like GOOD/BAD/BIG/SMALL). The concept requires reference to 'formal rank', 'monarch', 'state', 'ceremony', 'feudal tenure' — all institutional abstractions outside the scope of grounding notes and explications. It belongs to the social/legal/historical sector, not this tier.

Fifty is a grammatical number word (cardinal numeral). Number words name positions in an abstract infinite sequence and denote cardinality via a formal system (the natural numbers), not via perceivable properties or grounded relations expressible in the prime lexicon. While one can express 'there are many things' or 'two people', the specific identity of the numeral fifty—its position in the counting sequence, its arithmetic role, its conventional written and spoken forms—lies in the domain of formal mathematics and linguistic convention, not in concrete perceptual or bodily grounding. The prime lexicon provides only 'one', 'two', 'some', 'all', 'many', 'much', 'few', 'little' as quantity expressions; these are rough semantic primitives for approximate cardinality. Precise numerals like fifty belong to the symbolic/formal sector, outside the scope of this tier's grounding apparatus.

'technically' is an adverbial epistemic qualifier that modifies the truth-conditions or frame of reference of a whole clause or discourse move (e.g. 'technically, X is true but practically Y'). It expresses a meta-level stance about how a statement relates to formal definition, specialized knowledge, or practical reality. This is a speech-act and discourse-pragmatic phenomenon, not a concrete thing, simple relation, or mental state expressible in the bare primes. The core sense—'according to precise formal definition or specialized framework, even if it conflicts with common usage'—requires quantification over contexts, definitions, and speaker intentions that cannot be captured by the allowed explication frame (InstanceSchema/WhenTrue/RelationalSchema). It belongs to the pragmatics/discourse tier, not the semantic-primitive tier.

'ii' is a proper symbol or abbreviation (Roman numeral, month code, language code, music notation symbol) rather than a concept with everyday sense grounded in perception, action, or common things. None of these uses express a unified semantic primitives meaning; they are notational conventions specific to their domains (mathematics, calendar, linguistics, music theory). The dominant sense shifts by context; there is no stable single everyday concept to formalise.

Mathematics is a system of abstract symbolic reasoning and proof about formal structures (numbers, shapes, sets, functions). Its identity conditions—logical validity, axiomaticity, abstract generality—cannot be grounded in the prime lexicon of concrete percepts, bodies, actions, and places. The everyday sense 'doing arithmetic' is tractable (someone computes using symbols), but 'mathematics' as a discipline involves meta-level concepts (proof, axiom, formal system, abstraction) that exceed the semantic depth available in molecule grounding or the explication frame. This belongs to the knowledge/academic sector, not this tier.

'Blah' is primarily an interjection and discourse particle with multiple loosely-related senses (mild frustration, dismissal of meaningless talk, vomiting sound, expression of malaise). The noun senses ('nonsense', 'fool', 'discomfort') are secondary and culture-bound slang derivations. No single dominant everyday sense exists that is stable across contexts and expressible in the prime lexicon without circularity (defining it requires synonyms like 'meaningless', 'dull', 'bored' which themselves need grounding). The interjection uses are performative/pragmatic rather than truth-conditional, falling outside the scope of kot-ast/1 explication or molecule grounding.

The dominant everyday sense of 'thirty' is a cardinal number—a pure mathematical concept that expresses a quantity or position in a count sequence. Numbers are abstract relational entities whose identity depends on their place in an infinite formal system, not on percepts, bodily actions, concrete things, kinds, or substances grounded in the prime lexicon. The NSM framework (and by extension the Kernel of Truth controlled vocabulary) treats numbers as grammar-like structural primitives, not as molecules or explicable via THINK/KNOW/SEE/DO predicates over concrete referents. Thirty cannot be anchored to a single grounding note in ALLOWED words or to any concrete molecule in the catalog; it belongs to formal mathematics, not the everyday semantic tier this lexicographer serves.

Latin is a proper name for a historical language community and its linguistic system. While 'language' itself might be explicable as a system of words and sounds people use to say things, the specific identity of Latin—as the particular tongue spoken by Romans in a specific time and place, with its own phonological, morphological, and lexical properties—requires either historical-cultural grounding (Latium, Rome, Roman Empire) or technical linguistic grounding (inflectional paradigms, case systems, etc.) that lies outside the prime lexicon and the available catalog. Latin is not a kind of thing, place, body part, action, or substance graspable through 'something', 'people', 'place', 'when', or the other primes; it is a culturally and historically specific artifact-system whose definition depends on proper nouns and technical categories the Kernel of Truth programme does not cover.

The dominant everyday sense of 'moron' is a pejorative insult denoting stupidity or contempt toward a person's intellect. While the archaic clinical sense (IQ 50–70) is a technical classification, the current everyday use is primarily an expressive social judgment—a speech act of mockery or disdain—rather than a stable property or state expressible in prime predicates. The core semantic content is evaluative and interpersonal (someone judges another person as contemptible for lacking wit), not a factual condition about the person themselves. This falls outside both molecule grounding (no concrete percept or bodily action anchors it) and explication (no combination of THINK, FEEL, KNOW, SAY or relational primes captures the performative force of calling someone a moron without reducing it to mere synonym-substitution). The concept is culture-bound slang with unstable reference and no denotation the KoT lexicon can stabilise.

'caroline' in its primary sense is a proper name (a feminine given name) or a historical artifact (a specific silver coin of 16th–18th century Italy). The artifact sense requires grounding in historical monetary systems, metallurgy, and economic context that the prime lexicon and available catalog cannot express. The concept is culture-bound, time-specific, and depends on technical numismatic identity conditions (weight, mint, reign, alloy composition) that fall outside everyday perceptual or bodily grounding.

'southern' is a relative-spatial adjective whose meaning depends entirely on a frame of reference (a region, country, or speaker's location). The everyday sense 'of or in the south (relative to some implicit center)' cannot be grounded in the prime lexicon without access to a specific geographic or contextual anchor. It is fundamentally a deixis-dependent modifier, not a thing, action, state, or relation expressible in bare primes. The catalog contains no geographic entities (no 'Europe', no 'America') to anchor it, and introducing them would require technical-geographic grounding outside the Kernel tier.

'divine' is fundamentally a property-of-properties concept that conflates multiple incommensurable senses (pertaining to gods; transcendental; of surpassing excellence; beautiful; foretelling). The core sense—'of or pertaining to a god'—requires a prior definition of 'god' as a culturally and theologically laden concept with no stable grounding in the prime lexicon or available catalog. The secondary senses (eternal, holy, superhuman excellence, beauty) are evaluative and comparative adjectives that anchor to culture-specific ideals (divinity, sacredness, transcendence) rather than to perceivable actions, body parts, substances, or concrete things. The verbal sense (to foretell by divination) is equally culture-bound. No single everyday sense exists independent of theological or evaluative framework; any attempt to ground one would either trivialize it (e.g., 'people think something is very good') or require new molecules for 'god', 'sacred', 'transcendent'—each of which is itself theologically intractable at this tier.

'Julian' is a proper name (given name or calendar adjective 'Julian calendar'). Proper names are not in scope for semantic-primitive lexicography — they denote specific individuals or historical conventions rather than kinds, properties, or events expressible in the prime lexicon. The 'Kernel of Truth' programme defines everyday concepts with stable sense across speakers; proper names lack this universality and require external reference (identity tables, historical records) rather than semantic decomposition.

Pornography is an adult-oriented cultural artefact defined by its social/legal status (adults-only content intended for sexual arousal) and its pragmatic function (to produce a specific mental state in a consumer). Its identity conditions rest on intentionality (made *for* sexual arousal), social convention (age restriction), and subjective aesthetic/sexual response — all of which lie outside the prime lexicon. The concept cannot be grounded in perceivable physical properties, bodily actions, or concrete kinds; it requires metalinguistic reference to legality, adult intent, and sexual-pleasure semantics that the kernel does not support. It belongs to the adult-culture/legal sector, not the everyday-object tier.

marshall is a spelling variant (alternative spelling) of marshal, not a distinct concept with its own grounding. The dominant sense of marshal itself — a law-enforcement officer, military rank, or ceremonial official — requires either technical/institutional grounding (law enforcement, military hierarchy) or proper-name contexts (Marshal of France) that fall outside the prime lexicon and everyday semantic primitives. The concept's identity depends on social roles and formal institutions not expressible in the allowed words or catalog concepts.

Fifteen is a cardinal number—a pure abstract mathematical object whose identity depends on its place in an infinite ordered sequence (14 < 15 < 16) and its arithmetic properties (successor of 14, predecessor of 16). These relations cannot be expressed in the prime lexicon, which grounds only concrete things, kinds, actions, percepts, and relations over bodies and physical space. The concept requires formal mathematical structure (successorship, cardinality axioms) that lies outside the semantic-primitive tier. Numbers belong to the formal/logical layer, not the everyday grounding layer this lexicon serves.

'slut' is a sexual slur and pejorative term whose primary function is social condemnation rather than descriptive reference. Its dictionary senses conflate two incompatible semantic layers: (1) a descriptive claim about sexual behaviour (which would need to ground 'promiscuous' — itself requiring cultural norms about frequency/partner-count that are not universal or prime-expressible), and (2) a social judgment of shame/impropriety that is fundamentally evaluative and speaker-positioned, not a stable state-of-affairs. The term's 'everyday' use is overwhelmingly as a gendered insult; the rare descriptive uses are parasitic on that primary function. Since the kernel lexicon contains only GOOD/BAD as evaluative primes (not shame, stigma, or culturally-specific sexual norms), and since the concept's identity is inseparable from its function as a slur rather than a neutral category, formalisation would either (a) misrepresent it as a neutral descriptor it is not, or (b) require out-of-scope cultural/moral predicates. This belongs to the sociolinguistics and pragmatics domain, not the semantic-primitive tier.

'goddess' is a proper-noun category and cultural-religious role concept that depends on a prior notion of 'deity' (a supernatural being worshipped or prayed to) and 'female' (a sex category tied to reproductive biology and social role). While 'female' can be grounded in body and kinship primes, 'deity' itself requires a theory of the sacred, supernatural agency, and human worship — concepts that lie outside the prime lexicon and the catalog of available kernel and molecule references. The everyday senses (a female god; a beautiful woman honored; a woman of influence) all presuppose either supernatural status or social/aesthetic evaluation that cannot be expressed without those prior anchors. To formalize 'goddess' would require first formalizing 'deity', 'sacred', and 'worship', none of which are in the available catalog.

Admiral is a formal rank title in military hierarchies (naval commander of a specific rank grade). The concept requires grounding in institutional structure (navy, rank system, formal appointment) that the catalog cannot express. The everyday sense still hinges on this formal institutional role—unlike dog or house, which are grounded in visible kinds and properties. The allowed vocabulary has no way to express 'rank', 'military', 'navy', 'command authority', or 'appointment' without those catalog molecules or kernel concepts. Downgrading to a simpler paraphrase (e.g. 'someone who tells ships what to do') would lose the essential everyday meaning, which is specifically about formal naval command, not generic leadership. This is culture-bound institutional terminology requiring technical grounding.

'Norman' is primarily a proper name (Norman people, Norman conquest, personal name); the noun senses in the dictionary are either archaic/technical (a wooden bar or iron pin, a specialized term in construction or heraldry with no stable everyday use) or modern slang (normie, a culture-bound colloquialism). The everyday sense of 'normie' itself — roughly 'a person of conventional taste' — is grounded in social comparison and normativity that requires implicit reference to peer groups and cultural norms outside the prime lexicon. The architectural/technical sense has no foothold in the ALLOWED words or catalog. Neither sense is stably expressible via the primitive vocabulary or available molecule/kernel concepts.

Ambassador is a social-institutional role defined by official appointment, delegation of authority, and representation within a complex political/organizational structure. Its identity conditions—what makes someone an ambassador rather than a messenger, what 'representing a state' means in law and protocol, the authority to speak for another—depend on institutional facts and social convention that cannot be grounded in the prime lexicon (SOMEONE, PEOPLE, DO, KNOW, etc.) or the available catalog concepts. The core meaning 'one who is sent to speak/act for another entity in a formal capacity' requires concepts of authority, legitimacy, and delegation that belong to the social-institutional sector, not the perceptual/bodily/event tier this lexicon serves.

Madness is a multi-faceted psychiatric and emotional state concept that lacks stable definitional grounding in the prime lexicon. Its dominant everyday senses — (1) severe mental disorder involving loss of rational thought and behaviour control, (2) intense anger, (3) reckless folly — each require appeal to either technical psychiatric categories (psychosis, delusion, incoherence) not expressible in primes, or bundled emotional+cognitive+volitional states (wanting things that conflict with reality, feeling very bad, thinking things that are not true) that cannot be cleanly separated into atomic KNOW/THINK/WANT/FEEL roles. The concept sprawls across epistemic breakdown, affective extremity, and behavioural dyscontrol in ways the prime lexicon treats as separable. Attempted explosion into primes (someone thinks things very not true, feels very bad, cannot do what they want) either over-generalises (covers many milder conditions) or requires unstatable qualitative thresholds. This belongs to the clinical-psychology sector, not the everyday semantic tier.

wisdom is a complex dispositional property that requires meta-level reasoning about knowledge, virtue, context, and long-term consequences. It involves knowing when and how to apply knowledge across novel situations, balancing competing values (intrapersonal, interpersonal, institutional), and acting for 'common good'—all of which require either explicit quantification over propositional knowledge (outside the prime lexicon) or abstract institutional/ethical concepts not grounded in the catalog. The everyday sense conflates a stable trait (character), a situated competence (applying knowledge wisely in context), and normative social judgment (what counts as wise). None of these can be expressed as a molecule (concrete thing or action) or as an explication using only the available primes and catalog concepts without collapsing the meaning to 'someone knows many things and can do things well', which loses the core sense of discretionary judgment under uncertainty and the balancing of competing goods.

Soda in its dominant everyday sense refers to a commercially manufactured carbonated sweetened beverage. The definition requires 'carbonated' (a process state not in ALLOWED), 'sweetened' (a property not in ALLOWED), 'beverage/drink' (not in catalog or ALLOWED), and 'bubbles' or equivalent (not expressible). The catalog contains {urn:molecule-v0:water|water} [m] and {urn:molecule-v0:food|food} [m], but neither captures the specific manufactured artifact or the processes (carbonation, sweetening with sugar/syrup) that define soda. A grounding note cannot be constructed from the ALLOWED vocabulary (think, know, want, feel, see, hear, say, do, move, happen, live, die, is, be) plus structural references without introducing non-catalog content words like 'sweet', 'bubbly', 'flavor', 'caffeine', or 'drink'. This requires technical/scientific grounding (chemistry of CO₂ dissolution, food chemistry) or a catalog molecule for 'beverage' or 'carbonation' that does not exist.

'Sonny' is a proper name (given name/nickname) and a discourse particle (familiar form of address). Proper names lack stable sense conditions expressible in primes—their reference is fixed by social convention and naming history, not by grounding in percepts, actions, or kinds. Forms of address ('boy', 'man', 'express contempt/warning') are speech-act uses dependent on speaker intent and social context, not truth-conditional properties. The concept belongs to the onomastics and pragmatics domains, not to the everyday sense tier this lexicon serves.

Valentine is a culture-bound artefact whose identity depends on a specific calendar date (February 14th) and a social-conventional ritual of expressing romantic affection via greeting cards or gifts. The everyday sense conflates two distinct referents: (1) the physical object (card, gift, message) and (2) the person receiving or giving it. Neither can be grounded in the prime lexicon without explicit reference to the calendar convention, romantic social practice, or specific material forms (cards, gifts) that are not themselves in the catalog. The concept requires either sociocultural context (which is not expressible in primes) or a molecule anchoring to specific artefact kinds (e.g., greeting cards, gifts) that would themselves need grounding. It is neither a natural kind nor a bare action, state, or relation, and sits at the intersection of convention and material culture where the kernel lexicon cannot reach.

Radar is a technical apparatus whose identity conditions depend on electromagnetic physics (radio wave propagation, reflection, signal processing, Doppler shift) and engineering specifications (frequency, power, antenna design, signal-to-noise thresholds) that lie entirely outside the prime lexicon and the kernel catalog. The concept requires grounding in physics-domain primitives (electromagnetic field, wavelength, frequency) and technical measurement concepts (ranging, velocity determination) that cannot be expressed using only ALLOWED words, the available molecule/kernel references, or their combinations. It belongs to the technical/physics sector, not the everyday-percept tier that this catalog serves.

'boo' is a prototypical interjection and discourse particle with multiple distinct senses (startlement cry, audience disapproval, term of endearment, slang for cannabis). The dominant everyday senses are all speech acts or vocatives that either lack stable truth-conditional content (the startlement interjection has no propositional meaning beyond performative social effect) or are culture-bound slang (endearment, drug slang) whose grounding would require semantic primitives outside the kernel lexicon. The noun sense 'derisive shout' is parasitic on the interjection. None is expressible in the prime vocabulary or as a closed grounding note over the available catalog without semantic drift or circularity.

'prom' is a culture-bound social institution (a formal dance event for high school graduates) whose identity and significance rest on institutional and cultural context — graduation ceremonies, school systems, formal dress codes, social rituals — that cannot be grounded in the prime lexicon or expressed as a molecule over available catalog concepts. While the physical event (people gather, move together, music plays) is concrete, what makes it a 'prom' rather than merely 'a dance' is the social meaning and institutional framing, which lies outside the scope of semantic primitives.

'mo' is a clipping/informal abbreviation (colloquial shorthand for 'moment'), a slang term ('a homosexual'), or a symbol (ISO language code, month abbreviation). None of these are stable everyday concept-senses grounded in percepts, bodily actions, or concrete things. A clipping of a temporal unit might anchor to the kernel concept of time/moment, but 'mo' itself as a standalone form is primarily a discourse particle or informal register marker, not a concept the prime lexicon can ground. The word belongs to the grammar/register layer, not the semantic-primitive tier.

Medication denotes a substance (a drug) whose identity and effects depend on its chemical composition and pharmacological action—concepts that require technical scientific grounding (biochemistry, pharmacology) beyond the prime lexicon. While the everyday sense is 'something someone takes to make a bad feeling or broken body part become good', the definition of which substance counts as medication, what it does inside the body, and why it works all rest on causal mechanisms (receptor binding, enzyme inhibition, etc.) and chemical taxonomies that cannot be expressed using only primes like THING, BODY, GOOD, BAD, HAPPEN, and the available molecule catalog (which contains only food, not pharmaceuticals). A grounding note could say 'someone eats or drinks one kind of thing to make a bad feeling or broken body become good' — but this collapses medication into food or poison, losing the category boundary entirely. The catalog would need molecule entries for recognized drug classes (antibiotic, analgesic, etc.) anchored to their mechanisms; until those exist, medication remains ungroundable.

'Dope' has multiple distinct senses across different registers (lubricant/coating in manufacturing; recreational drugs; performance-enhancing substances in sport; verb 'to drug' or 'to treat with chemical substance'). The dominant everyday sense varies by context and speaker community: in medical/sports contexts it means 'prohibited performance-enhancing drug'; in street/colloquial English it can mean 'heroin' or 'marijuana'; in technical contexts it means 'lubricant or coating material'; in verb form it means 'to add a dopant to a semiconductor' or 'to treat with drugs'. No single 'everyday sense' is stable across English-speaking communities. The concept lacks a unified semantic core expressible in primes or as a molecule grounding note, and its primary modern uses (drug names, technical chemistry) belong to technical or subcultural registers, not the shared semantic commons this tier aims to capture.

Indistinctly is an adverb modifying the manner of perception or cognition (seeing, hearing, knowing indistinctly). Its core meaning — lacking sharp distinctions or clarity — is a property of how something appears or is known, not grounded in concrete percepts, bodily actions, artefacts, or places. While 'distinct' might be explicated as a relational property (parts are separable; boundaries are clear), 'indistinctly' as an adverbial modifier of manner falls outside the scope of the prime lexicon: it would require abstract predicates over the quality of perceptual or cognitive acts (e.g., 'perceive in a blurred way') that cannot be reduced to the allowed substantive and action primes without circular appeal to synonyms like 'blurred', 'unclear', or 'fuzzy'. The concept belongs to the epistemic/phenomenological register, not the sensorimotor tier this formalisation targets.

A prosecutor is a social role defined within a legal system—a person who holds an institutional position to represent the state in criminal proceedings. The concept's identity depends on cultural-legal infrastructure (courts, laws, government authority, the adversarial or inquisitorial system) that lies outside the prime lexicon. While we can say 'someone does something' or 'someone speaks words', the specific institutional meaning—what makes a person a prosecutor rather than merely 'someone who says the state did wrong'—requires reference to legal concepts (criminal law, the state as entity, formal accusation procedures) that are not available as molecules, kernel concepts, or expressible in bare primes. This belongs to the legal/institutional sector, not the everyday-sense semantic tier.

'Irish' is a proper adjective derived from the place name Ireland and primarily denotes nationality, ethnic identity, or cultural affiliation — all culture-bound categories that lack stable grounding in the prime lexicon or kernel concepts. While one could attempt to define 'of or from Ireland' using place references, Ireland itself is a proper geographical entity not representable as a molecule or kernel concept in this system. The everyday senses (nationality, ethnicity, language group, cultural membership) all presuppose social/political institutions and historical contingencies outside the scope of semantic primitives. This concept belongs to the anthropology and geopolitics sector, not the tier of grounded perception and bodily action.

'creative' in its dominant everyday senses requires either a property frame (ability to make novel things in an excellent way) or a dispositional characterization (tending to do so) that cannot be reduced to the prime lexicon without circularity. The concept involves evaluative judgments about novelty and excellence ('in a novel fashion', 'excellently') that are not expressible in bare primes (GOOD, BAD, BIG, SMALL), and the notion of 'ability to create' requires either a forward-looking modal complement (CAN-DO something not yet specified) or a kind-based property (being of the creative kind) that collapses back into synonymy. The adjective senses all presuppose an implicit comparison class or standard of evaluation ('original', 'imaginative', 'novel') that sits outside the kernel. The noun senses are occupational or artifact roles, which could be grounded, but they are secondary to the dominant adjective use in everyday English, and that adjective use cannot be formalized without technical vocabulary (e.g. 'generates novel outputs', 'shows originality') that lies beyond the allowed primes.

'soviet' is a proper institutional and political term whose everyday sense requires grounding in 20th-century history (Russian Revolution, communist governance structures, the USSR state itself). The concept denotes either a specific type of workers' council or the Soviet Union as a geopolitical entity. Neither can be expressed using the prime lexicon alone: a soviet as a 'council of workers' would need terms like 'workers', 'council', 'communist', 'Russia' — none of which are in the allowed vocabulary or available as kernel/molecule references. The adjective sense (pertaining to the Soviet Union) is equally culture-bound and proper-name-dependent. This belongs to the history/politics sector, not the tier of semantic primitives.

Baron is a social-institutional rank defined by hereditary succession, legal privilege, and feudal hierarchy — concepts rooted in historical European political systems. Its identity conditions depend on membership in a formal nobility system, delegation of authority from a monarch, and property tenure (fief) that are not expressible in the prime lexicon or as a grounding note over the available catalog. The concept requires technical-institutional grounding (heraldry, feudal law, succession rules) that belongs to the history-and-institutions sector, not the everyday perceptual/bodily/relational tier that this lexicon covers.

'Mexican' is a proper demonym (adjective/noun denoting nationality or ethnic origin) anchored to a specific geopolitical entity and cultural history. It does not denote a concrete thing, a stable kind of thing, a body part, a percept, a bodily action, an artefact, a place, a substance, or a time of day that can be grounded in the prime lexicon. The everyday senses ('a person from Mexico', 'relating to Mexico or Mexican culture') require reference to a particular nation-state and its cultural practices, which lie outside the semantic primitives and the available catalog. It is a culture-bound proper descriptor, not a universal concept expressible in semantic primes.

Gunfire refers to the discharge of guns — a technological artifact whose operation depends on gunpowder, metallurgy, and mechanical engineering that lie outside the prime lexicon and the catalog of grounded concepts. The core sense involves a complex causal chain (ignition of powder → projectile acceleration → sound generation) that cannot be decomposed into the available primitives (HAPPEN, MOVE, SEE, HEAR, etc.) without smuggling in the concept 'gun' itself, which is not in the catalog and would require grounding. The everyday sense is inseparable from this artifact-dependent structure.

The noun sense 'wife' is an archaic/obsolete English term with no stable modern everyday use; the verb sense is a technical chemistry term (Dutch process for cocoa treatment) that requires domain-specific grounding. Neither sense is expressible in the prime lexicon or catalog concepts. The capitalized proper adjective 'Dutch' (relating to the Netherlands) is a cultural/national category that falls outside semantic primitives. The concept lacks a dominant everyday sense grounded in percepts, bodily actions, or concrete things.

'Superman' is a proper name and fictional character from a specific cultural narrative (DC Comics, created 1938). While the term also carries a philosophical sense ('übermensch', a superior human type), both the proper-name reading and the abstract philosophical concept exceed the scope of everyday grounding. The everyday sense conflates a trademarked character identity with a general idea of 'superhuman person', which cannot be stably defined in the prime lexicon without either collapsing to 'someone with very good things' (losing the superhuman/extraordinary dimension) or introducing culture-specific narrative scaffolding (comic books, costumes, secret identities) that belongs outside this tier. The concept belongs to fiction-studies and philosophy sectors, not the perceptual-grounding tier.

'European' is a proper-adjective (demonym) denoting membership in a geopolitical category defined by the continent of Europe and modern political institutions (EU, nation-states). Its identity conditions rest on historical, legal, and cartographic facts that cannot be grounded in the prime lexicon or the available catalog concepts (which cover percepts, body parts, artefacts, and basic actions). The everyday sense conflates geography, citizenship, cultural identity, and political belonging — all load-bearing but external to semantic primitives. A stable grounding would require references to nations, borders, legal systems, and historical events that lie outside the 'Kernel of Truth' tier.

'Bonnie' is primarily a proper name (Scottish feminine given name) with secondary adjectival uses that are either Scottish dialect ('merry, happy, beautiful') or highly culture-specific (bonfire celebration). The adjective senses are not core everyday English vocabulary in most speech communities—they are marked as dialectal or archaic. The dominant modern sense in English dictionaries is as a given name, which falls outside the scope of this lexicographer (proper names are not definable in prime lexicon). The non-name adjectival senses ('merry', 'beautiful', 'good') would reduce to kernel concepts like GOOD or already-known emotional/aesthetic properties, but the name-first status and Scottish-dialect marking prevent a stable, non-culture-bound grounding.

Thanksgiving is a culture-bound proper noun (a specific national holiday with a fixed calendar date) and a mass noun denoting a cultural practice whose identity depends on historical convention, legal designation, and collective social agreement rather than on percepts, bodily actions, artefacts, or stable semantic primitives. The core sense — 'expression of gratitude' — is a speech act and mental state that requires EXPLICATION, but the dominant everyday usage conflates this with a specific named event (Thanksgiving Day in the US/Canada on a particular date), a historical institution, and a secular social ritual. That institutional identity cannot be grounded in the prime lexicon or the kernel catalog. The grounding-note route fails because 'a national holiday' and 'harvest festival' are not in ALLOWED. The explication route would capture only the generic gratitude-speech sense, not the culturally entrenched holiday sense that dominates current English use. Honest classification: cannot-formalise.

'Korean' is a proper adjective and demonym denoting membership in or relation to a nation-state (Korea) and its people, language, and culture. 'benny' in its dominant everyday senses is either a proper name (Benny as a given name or nickname for Benjamin) or slang with narrow sociolinguistic scope (amphetamine tablet, tantrum, money denomination). Franklin is a historical socioeconomic rank (14th–15th century English gentry class) anchored in specific feudal land-tenure relations and institutional contexts that have no stable modern everyday sense. The concept requires grounding in domain-specific historical/legal structures (freeholder status, gentry hierarchy, medieval property law) that are not expressible in the prime lexicon or as references to available kernel/molecule concepts. 'Spiritual' is a property concept that anchors to metaphysical or religious domains ('of or pertaining to the spirit or soul', 'pertaining to God', 'supernatural', 'incorporeal') which lack grounding in the prime lexicon's concrete percepts, bodily actions, and observable states. Cocaine is a specific chemical substance whose identity and effects depend on molecular pharmacology (CNS stimulant tropane alkaloid, vasoconstriction, addiction mechanism, local anesthetic properties) that cannot be grounded in the prime lexicon or existing catalog concepts. Its definitional content requires technical chemistry and neuropharmacology — receptor binding, alkaloid structure, dopamine reuptake inhibition — none of which are expressible as relations among ALLOWED words or extant molecules. This belongs to the medical/chemistry sector, not the tier of grounding-note primitives.

'Physically' is an adverb expressing manner or modality — how an action is performed or a state holds (in the physical world, using bodily force, via direct contact rather than remote means). Its core sense is a meta-linguistic operator modifying other predicates or clauses, not a standalone concept with identity conditions. To define it would require operators over manner/modality that exceed the prime lexicon's scope. The catalog kernel contains no manner adverbials or modality markers, only base predicates (MOVE, DO, etc.) and their standard roles. A grounding note cannot express 'in a physical manner' without smuggling in the concept it should define.

bingo is primarily a game that requires formal rules, turn-taking structure, and scorekeeper roles that fall outside the everyday descriptive vocabulary available. while 'game' and 'win' are common English words, they are not in the allowed lexicon and cannot be re-expressed using only {things, people, touch, move, say, hear, moment, place}. the concept's core meaning depends on understanding an arbitrary system of rules and winners, which requires either those grounding words or reference to a kernel concept like {urn:kernel-v0:kind|kind} — but 'kind of game' still fails because 'game' itself is not allowed and no kernel game concept exists. bingo also carries strong interjection senses (expressing discovery or agreement) that are discourse particles outside the scope of formalisation. the everyday descriptive sense cannot be gate-legally expressed with the available primitives.

'Excellency' has two senses in the provided text: (1) a synonym for excellence (the quality of being excellent), which is itself an abstract property requiring grounding in evaluative primes not available in the allowed lexicon; and (2) an honorific title or style conferred on high officials and aristocrats, which is a culture-bound, institutional convention tied to specific governance structures and social hierarchies. Neither sense is anchored in the concrete, perceptual, or bodily primitives required for a molecule, nor can either be fully expressed using only the allowed primes (GOOD, BAD, and their cognates are too coarse to capture the normative judgment 'excellent' without circular reference back to excellency itself). The honorific sense is especially intractable: it requires concepts like 'office', 'tenure', 'courtesy', and 'sovereign state' that belong to institutional/legal domains outside the scope of this tier.

A poet is defined by a volitional social role (someone who writes poems; someone identified as such by community practice) and by dispositional properties (creative or romantic imagination). The core sense requires either (i) a grounding in the practice of {urn:kernel-v0:make|make} applied to {urn:molecule-v0:book|book} [m] of a specific cultural kind (poems, which are linguistic artefacts with formal/aesthetic properties not in the prime lexicon), or (ii) a mental-state attribution (romantic imagination) that cannot be reduced to the allowed primes without circular appeal to 'creativity' or 'poetry'. The social-role reading requires reference to community recognition and cultural convention, which lie outside the scope of the kernel. Neither the grounding-note route nor explication can anchor this concept without importing culture-specific knowledge or undefined higher-order predicates.

The dominant everyday sense of 'operator' is a person whose role or job is to run/control a device, system, or service. While 'person' is a prime, the concept's identity rests on role and instrumental relation (X operates Y), which requires either a stable kinship/occupational category (like 'teacher' or 'surgeon' in the kernel) or a relational explication that names the specific domain (telecommunications, machinery, military). Without a grounding molecule for 'device' or 'system' in the catalog, and without an already-catalogued occupational kernel concept that captures 'one who operates X', the role-based identity cannot be anchored in the available lexicon or as a compositional molecule. The term is semantically transparent only relative to its object (operator-of-what?), making it a domain-dependent category rather than a concrete thing, body part, or stable substance.

'Sixth' is a grammatical ordinal number word and a structural position marker (like 'first', 'second'). Its identity rests on numerical sequence and place-value within an ordered series—concepts that belong to the abstract mathematical domain, not the grounded perceptual-action tier the prime lexicon serves. The ordinal sense ('the sixth position') requires formal arithmetic; the fractional sense ('one of six equal parts') requires divisibility as an abstract relation; and the musical sense requires domain-specific pitch organization. None of these have stable grounding in the allowed molecule-building materials (concrete things, body parts, bodily actions, places, substances, perceptible events). A child learns 'sixth' by counting or by memorizing position in a sequence, not by perceiving a thing or action in the world. It belongs to the grammar-and-number sector, not this tier.

A journalist is defined by a complex occupational role (gathering information, processing it into newsworthy form, disseminating to public) that depends on culturally specific institutions (press, news media), social conventions about what counts as 'newsworthy', and abstractly defined professional practices. The core actions (gathering, processing, disseminating information) are expressible in primes, but 'journalist' as a kind of person is identity-constituted by these institutional and conventional roles, not by any physical, perceptual, or behavioural grounding that the prime lexicon can anchor. The concept belongs to the social-institutional sector, not the everyday percept-and-action tier.

'African' is a proper demonym (adjective and noun referring to a person from, or something of, the continent Africa). It names a geopolitical region and the people inhabiting it. Identity conditions for membership rest on geographical, cultural, and political facts that are not expressible in the prime lexicon (which contains no place names, nations, or cultural group markers beyond the bare primes PEOPLE, PLACE, WHEN, and WHERE). The concept requires either a proper-name slot or encyclopedic grounding outside the closed semantic primitives; it does not reduce to a composition of ALLOWED words or catalog concepts.

Chess is a complex rule-governed game with culturally-specific identity conditions (the board structure, piece types, move rules, and win conditions). While its components—the board, pieces, players—are concrete, the concept of 'chess' itself is defined by an intricate system of formal rules that cannot be decomposed into prime predicates or grounded in the available catalog without either trivializing the definition or inventing new molecule slots for dozens of game-rule concepts (piece types, move patterns, checkmate, etc.). It belongs to the cultural/ludic domain, not to the tier of perception, bodily action, natural kinds, and artefacts that the prime lexicon supports.

'Psycho' as a colloquial adjective/noun referring to mental illness or dangerous behaviour requires grounding in clinical or psychiatric concepts (psychosis, psychopathy, antisocial personality) that are not expressible in the prime lexicon. The everyday slang sense conflates distinct diagnostic categories and relies on cultural stereotypes about mental illness. A rigorous definition would need either (a) technical psychiatric grounding unavailable in this catalog, or (b) a decomposition into observable behavioural primitives (e.g. 'acts in ways that people fear'), which would lose the psychiatric meaning entirely and reduce to a generic negative-evaluation stereotype. The psychology-class sense is technical jargon. None of these senses ground stably in ALLOWED words or catalog molecules.

'Ahem' is a discourse particle and interjection whose primary function is pragmatic: to signal speaker intention (drawing attention, expressing disapproval, signalling insincerity or discomfort) or to perform a social action (mild throat-clearing). While it has a weak physical correlate (sound of throat-clearing), the dominant everyday sense is not the sound itself but the communicative act—a speech-act performative. Speech acts and pragmatic intentions are not expressible in the prime lexicon or the available kernel/molecule catalog, which grounds only concrete things, events, physical actions, and simple propositional contents. The interjection cannot be reduced to a thing that happens, a state someone is in, or a relation between objects without evacuating its meaning.

'Sonic' is a relational adjective denoting a property of things (sound-related) or speeds (approaching sound speed). The dominant everyday sense—'of or relating to sound'—requires the technical concept SOUND, which is not available in the prime lexicon or catalog. Sound as a physical phenomenon (wave propagation, frequency, vibration detection) belongs to the physics/sensory sector and cannot be grounded in the allowed primes (which include HEAR but not SOUND as an object or substance). The speed-based sense is even more technical. Neither sense is expressible as a molecule (no concrete grounding thing or action) nor as a pure explication over available primes and kernel concepts.

'tech' is a colloquial abbreviation with multiple senses (technology, technician, technique, the computing industry) that lacks a single stable everyday referent. The dominant sense—'technology' or 'computing/Internet business'—is itself a mass/abstract collective noun denoting a domain of human activity and artefacts rather than a concrete thing, kind, body part, percept, bodily action, artefact, place, substance, or time. It cannot be grounded in a molecule (no sensory anchor or concrete particular), and its explication would require predicates like CAUSE, MAKE, KNOW, and abstract relational operators not reducible to the kernel primes without collapsing into near-synonymy ('ways people do things'). The concept belongs to institutional/economic discourse, not the perceptual-action tier this lexicon serves.

'ms' is a symbolic abbreviation with multiple technical/specialized senses (millisecond unit, ISO language code, manuscript abbreviation, plural marker). None of these dominant senses can be grounded in the prime lexicon or catalog molecules without introducing technical measurement terminology (SI units, time quantification below human perception), language metadata (ISO codes), or meta-linguistic reference (abbreviation, plural). The everyday sense — if any — is context-dependent and fragmentary rather than a unified concept with stable identity conditions. This belongs to the technical/symbolic tier, not the everyday-concept tier.

'Psychic' as ordinarily used refers to a person who claims to possess paranormal or extrasensory abilities (telepathy, clairvoyance, contact with the dead, etc.). These abilities are not grounded in observable physical action, perceivable sensation, or bodily capacity expressible in the prime lexicon. The concept depends on supernatural or parapsychological premises that fall outside the scope of semantic primitives anchored in everyday perceivable events, body, movement, and speech. It belongs to the domain of folk belief and pseudoscience rather than to concrete, observable kinds of people and their ordinary actions.

'zombie' in its dominant everyday sense denotes a being created through fictional or supernatural reanimation—either magical (Haitian folklore) or science-fictional (modern horror media: fungal infection, viral reanimation, radiation, etc.). The concept's identity conditions rest on narrative/genre conventions and fictional agency mechanisms that have no stable grounding in the prime lexicon. Unlike 'alive' or 'dead' (which anchor to bodily states and {urn:kernel-v0:alive|alive}/{urn:kernel-v0:dead|dead}), a zombie is defined by its mode of origin (reanimation via magic or speculative biological means) and cultural context, not by observable properties expressible in primes. The term conflates multiple incompatible fictional scenarios (Haitian magical reanimation vs. viral-outbreak fast-moving predators) and carries heavy genre-specific baggage. It belongs to the fictional-horror and folklore sectors, not the everyday-percept tier this catalog serves.

'electrical' is a technical-physics adjective denoting properties and phenomena that depend on electric charge, electromagnetic fields, and quantum electrodynamics. Its identity conditions (charge quantization, field behaviour, energy states of electrons in matter) belong to physics and cannot be grounded in the prime lexicon or as a molecule over the available catalog. The everyday colloquial sense 'relating to electricity' merely defers to the same technical domain. It is not a percept, body part, concrete artefact, substance, or action expressible in bare primes; it requires theoretical physics vocabulary absent from this tier.

'Instrumental' has multiple senses across distinct semantic domains: (1) a grammatical case marker for means/agency — a technical linguistic concept requiring formal syntax not expressible in primes; (2) adjective meaning 'essential/important' — an evaluative property that would need explicit grounding in what makes something 'central' to an outcome, which requires causal reasoning beyond the prime lexicon; (3) music without vocals — a concrete artefact sense, but 'music' itself is not in the catalog (urn:molecule-v0:music exists but is depth-1 and has no grounding), and 'vocals/singing' require linguistic agency concepts not primitively available. The everyday senses either require catalog expansion (music grounding) or belong to technical domains (grammar, evaluation theory) outside the current depth-4 molecule/kernel scope.

Brazil is a proper name—a specific geographic polity (the Federative Republic of Brazil)—not a general concept expressible in the prime lexicon. Proper names denote particular individuals in the world, not kinds or properties or events that can be grounded in everyday semantic primitives. The secondary senses (brazil wood, brazil nut, dye, coal seam) are technical/commercial subcategories and lack stable unified grounding. This belongs to the geography/political sector, not the semantic-primitive tier.

'samurai' is a culture-bound historical role-category specific to feudal Japan, defined by social position (retainer to a daimyo), legal privileges (weapon possession), training regimen, and honour codes that have no stable grounding in the prime lexicon. The concept requires reference to 'daimyo', 'feudal Japan', 'warrior class', 'swordsmanship', and institutional structures that cannot be decomposed into semantic primitives (someone, people, do, move, know, etc.) without circular appeals to the same cultural-historical framework. It belongs to the history and sociology sector, not the tier of everyday sensory/bodily/relational primitives.

'nazi' is a proper historical and political category whose identity conditions depend on specific 20th-century institutional, ideological, and biographical facts (membership in NSDAP, adherence to a particular ultranationalist fascist ideology, historical context of 1930s–1940s Germany) that cannot be expressed in the prime lexicon or grounded in the available catalog of concrete things, bodies, places, and basic actions. The term also carries heavy evaluative and political freight that resists decomposition into primitive mental states or relations. It belongs to the history and political theory sector, not this formalisation tier.

This word has multiple senses across regional registers (anatomical, insult-noun, insult-verb, slur) with no single dominant everyday sense stable across English-speaking communities. The anatomical sense is a body part (molecule-grounding territory), but it is culturally marked and taboo. The insult senses are speech acts or evaluative predicates that require either cultural/pragmatic grounding outside the prime lexicon (what makes someone 'objectionable' in one region vs. another) or direct reference to human harm/contempt, which cannot be expressed in primes without importing the very cultural judgment the word encodes. The verb senses reference either the taboo utterance itself (circular) or violent/social actions (betray, ruin, attack) whose preconditions are culture-bound. No kernel concept or available catalog molecule anchors the stable everyday meaning across all registers. Formalising only the anatomical sense would misrepresent the concept's actual function in English, where the insult senses dominate in most communities.

Eternity is a concept that requires quantification over infinite time or negation of temporal bounds ("time with no end"). The prime lexicon contains temporal anchors (NOW, WHEN~TIME, MOMENT, A-LONG-TIME, A-SHORT-TIME, FOR-SOME-TIME) but no primitive for infinity, unboundedness, or negation of temporal termination. An explication would need to express either "time that does not end" (requiring a negated END operator applied to time itself) or "existing outside time" (requiring a meta-temporal frame not available in kot-ast/1). Both exceed the expressive closure of profile-1 grammar and the allowed predicate set. Eternity belongs to metaphysical/mathematical discourse, not the everyday perceptual-behavioural tier this lexicon grounds.

The dominant everyday sense of 'idol' refers to a culturally or socially constructed object of admiration—either a physical representation (statue, image) or a person (celebrity, entertainer) regarded with reverence or intense devotion. This concept requires grounding in social/cultural practices ('people admire this', 'people want to be like this'), personal emotional attachment ('someone feels something very good about this'), and often intentional representation or curation of an image or persona. While admiration, feeling, and social acts are in principle expressible via FEEL and KNOW predicates, 'idol' as such is fundamentally a role or status within a social system (fan culture, religious practice, celebrity construction) that has no stable physical or behavioural anchor independent of human cultural interpretation. The concept cannot be reduced to percepts, bodily actions, or concrete artefacts without losing the essential social-intentional character that makes something an 'idol' rather than merely 'a statue' or 'a famous person'. It belongs to the social-cultural-institutional sector, not the tier of primes grounded in perception and bodily action.

'fortunately' is an adverbial discourse particle expressing the speaker's or narrator's evaluation or attitude toward a proposition or event. Its core sense — 'it is fortunate/good that X' — requires meta-level judgement about whether an event aligns with someone's interests or desires, which maps to speaker stance rather than to a grounded thing, action, or state in the prime lexicon. While {urn:kernel-v0:good} exists as a kernel concept and can be used to express that something has a positive property, 'fortunately' operates at the speech-act or narrative-commentary level (similar to 'unfortunately', 'sadly', 'wisely') to signal the narrator's or speaker's attitude. This exceeds what the molecule route can anchor (no grounding-note for speech-act stance), and the explication route would require a meta-clause about speaker belief or evaluation that lies outside the closed grammar of kot-ast/1. The concept belongs to the pragmatics/discourse tier, not the semantic-primitive tier.

pistol is a manufactured weapon whose functioning depends on combustive/ballistic physics (propellant ignition, projectile velocity, chamber pressure) that cannot be grounded in the allowed everyday vocabulary. The concept requires technical understanding of mechanisms (firing pin, chamber, barrel rifling) and physics (momentum, energy transfer) that lie outside the semantic primitives available in the kernel. While 'shooting' and related actions could theoretically be expressed, pistol's identity is fundamentally tied to its technical design and operation — it is not a natural kind or simple everyday object like dog, house, or food. The catalog lacks the machinery concepts (mechanism, spring, metal, explosive) needed to build even a minimal gate-legal grounding note.

'Ninja' is a proper cultural-historical category from pre-modern Japan (a spy/infiltrator role with specific social context), and its modern extended senses (programmer, corporate enforcer, metaphorical 'skillful person') are all borrowed from or analogical to that historical referent. The core identity—a person trained in stealth infiltration during Japan's shogunate—depends on spatiotemporal and cultural-institutional facts (feudal Japan, specific social structure, ninjutsu training tradition) that the prime lexicon cannot express. The everyday modern uses (tech worker, 'fast and sneaky') are playful reappropriations or slang that lack stable ground truth outside the historical sense. This is a culture-bound proper category, not a grounding-note concept.

The dominant everyday sense of 'wizard' is a person skilled in magic or mystical practices — a cultural/fantasy archetype anchored in storytelling traditions rather than observable human action, bodily capacity, or concrete artifact. Magic itself (spells, enchantments, supernatural causation) is not expressible in the prime lexicon: it requires intentional causation outside the bounds of physical action, perception, and ordinary social practice that the kernel primes (DO, THINK, WANT, MAKE, MOVE, SEE, HEAR) can capture. The secondary sense (expert in a technical field, e.g. 'network wizard') is a metaphorical extension of the magical sense and inherits the same barrier. The tertiary sense (computer program/wizard mode) is a technical-domain artefact sense, culture-bound to computing. None of these grounds in observable primitives or the available molecule/kernel catalog.

Manhattan is a proper noun referring to a specific geographic location (a borough of New York City). Proper nouns denote unique, historically contingent individuals rather than general concepts expressible in the prime lexicon. While the word can be used metonymically (e.g. 'the Manhattan cocktail' or cricket statistics chart), these are derivative senses dependent on the proper name. The everyday primary sense — the place itself — cannot be grounded in semantic primitives without access to geopolitical/historical facts external to the controlled lexicon.

'generally' is a discourse adverb and scope-modifier that expresses epistemic stance (approximation, typicality, or generalization over cases) rather than a grounded thing, action, or perceivable state. Its meaning depends entirely on the clause it modifies and cannot be decomposed into the prime lexicon (which offers only concrete things, bodies, basic actions, and simple mental states like KNOW, WANT, FEEL). To formalize 'generally' would require metalinguistic operators over propositions or quantifier scoping that lie outside kot-ast/1 profile-1 grammar and the semantic-primitive basis.

Enterprise has multiple senses spanning different conceptual types: (1) a concrete organizational entity (company, business), (2) an abstract undertaking or project, (3) a dispositional quality (willingness, initiative), and (4) a verbal sense (to undertake). The dominant everyday sense conflates organizational kind with purposeful activity in a way that requires either technical socio-institutional grounding (not in the prime lexicon) or a disjunction across multiple frames. A grounding note cannot express 'a human organization formed to do purposeful coordinated activity for material exchange' using only allowed primes and available catalog molecules/kernels. The concept belongs to the institutional/economic domain, which sits above the everyday physical and interpersonal tier that the current lexicon grounds.

'Mademoiselle' is a French social-courtesy title whose meaning depends on cultural conventions about marital status, linguistic identity, and formal address norms specific to French-speaking societies. The concept cannot be grounded in the prime lexicon (which lacks the social categories 'unmarried', 'married', 'French', or 'courtesy title') or reduced to the universal semantic primes of the Kernel of Truth. It is a culture-bound social category, not an everyday perceptual or bodily concept, and belongs to the sociolinguistic sector rather than this tier.

A website is a technical-digital construct defined by network infrastructure (domain names, web servers, URLs, HTTP protocols, hyperlinking) and computational organization that cannot be grounded in the prime lexicon of concrete percepts, bodies, bodily actions, and substances. Its identity depends on abstract technical properties (domain ownership, server location, URL structure) and digital content aggregation that lie outside the scope of everyday sensory and social primitives. The concept belongs to the digital/technical sector, not the tier of grounding this lexicon serves.

'Anyhow' is a discourse particle and connective adverb whose primary function is to signal logical or conversational relation (concession, resumption, topic shift) between clauses or turns.
